title: Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease
text:
Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease  
Andrea Belluzzi, Corrado Brignola, Massimo Campieri, Angelo Perà, Stefano Boschi, and Mario Miglioli  
New England Journal of Medicine, 1996;334:1557-1560  
DOI: 10.1056/NEJM199606133342401  

Background & Rationale  
Crohn’s disease is characterised by periods of remission interrupted by relapses, with the highest risk of relapse occurring soon after remission is achieved. Patients with elevated serum acute-phase proteins are at particularly high risk. Fish oil, rich in n-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, has demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties through mechanisms including reduced leukotriene B4 and thromboxane A2 production, inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and free radical scavenging. Despite theoretical benefits, conventional fish oil preparations are poorly tolerated due to gastrointestinal side effects and unpleasant taste, limiting their clinical utility. This study evaluated a novel enteric-coated fish-oil preparation designed to improve tolerability and absorption in patients with Crohn’s disease at high risk of relapse.  

Study Design  
This was a one-year, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial conducted at two Italian centres between May 1992 and September 1993. Seventy-eight patients with established Crohn’s disease in clinical remission (Crohn’s Disease Activity Index score ≤150 for at least three months but less than two years) were enrolled. High-risk status was defined by elevated serum a1-acid glycoprotein (>130 mg/dL), a2-globulin (>0.9 g/dL), or erythrocyte sedimentation rate (>40 mm/hour). Patients were excluded if they had received mesalamine, sulfasalazine, corticosteroids, or immunosuppressive drugs within specified timeframes, or had undergone extensive bowel resection. Patients were randomised to receive either nine enteric-coated capsules daily containing 2.7 g total n-3 fatty acids (1.8 g eicosapentaenoic acid and 0.9 g docosahexaenoic acid) or identical placebo capsules. The enteric coating resisted gastric acid for at least 30 minutes before disintegrating in the small intestine. The primary endpoint was clinical relapse, defined as an increase in Crohn’s Disease Activity Index score of at least 100 points above baseline and a score >150 for more than two weeks. Secondary endpoints included maintenance of remission at one year and changes in inflammatory markers. Randomisation was balanced-block, and all analyses were intention-to-treat.  

Patient Population  
The study population comprised 78 patients (39 per group) with a median age of 39 years (placebo) and 34 years (fish oil), with similar sex distribution and disease characteristics between groups. Most patients had ileal or ileocolonic disease involvement. The median duration of disease was 66 months in the placebo group and 68 months in the fish-oil group. Approximately one-third of patients in each group had undergone previous bowel resection. The median duration of remission before study entry was 7 months in the placebo group and 8 months in the fish-oil group. Baseline Crohn’s Disease Activity Index scores were comparable (median 82 vs 78).  

Key Findings  
At one year, 27 of 39 patients (69%) in the placebo group experienced relapse compared to 11 of 39 patients (28%) in the fish-oil group (difference 41 percentage points; 95% CI 21 to 61; P=0.001). Twenty-three patients (59%) in the fish-oil group remained in remission at one year versus 10 patients (26%) in the placebo group (P=0.003). Kaplan-Meier analysis confirmed significantly better maintenance of remission in the fish-oil group (P=0.006). Multivariate logistic regression identified fish-oil treatment as the only independent predictor of relapse (odds ratio 4.2; 95% CI 1.6 to 10.7), with no significant effects from sex, age, previous surgery, disease duration, or smoking status. Among patients who relapsed, the distribution of disease location and severity of symptoms were similar between groups.  

Safety & Tolerability  
Five patients (four in the fish-oil group, one in the placebo group) discontinued due to diarrhoea, which began within the first month of treatment and did not improve with dose reduction. No other adverse effects were reported. Laboratory markers of inflammation (erythrocyte sedimentation rate, a2-globulins, a1-acid glycoprotein) showed significant improvement in the fish-oil group compared to placebo. Analysis of red blood cell phospholipid fatty acid composition demonstrated incorporation of n-3 fatty acids and displacement of arachidonic acid in the fish-oil group.  

Authors' Conclusions  
The authors concluded that the enteric-coated fish-oil preparation significantly reduced the rate of relapse in patients with Crohn’s disease in remission, particularly those at high risk of relapse. They noted the preparation’s good tolerability and high compliance rates compared to conventional fish oil formulations. The authors acknowledged that the relative efficacy compared to standard treatments such as mesalamine remains to be determined.  

Reference  
Belluzzi A, Brignola C, Campieri M, Perà A, Boschi S, Miglioli M. Effect of an enteric-coated fish-oil preparation on relapses in Crohn’s disease. N Engl J Med 1996;334:1557-60.

title: **Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease**
text:
**Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease**
Belluzzi A, Brignola C, Campieri M, Pera A, Boschi S, Miglioli M. N Engl J Med 1996; 334:1557-60.
DOI: Not reported in the source publication.

**Background & Rationale**
Crohn’s disease is characterised by periods of remission interrupted by relapses, with the highest risk occurring shortly after remission is achieved, particularly in patients with persistent laboratory evidence of inflammation. Fish oil possesses anti-inflammatory properties, including inhibition of leukotriene and thromboxane synthesis and cytokine production, which are implicated in Crohn’s disease pathogenesis. However, conventional fish oil preparations are often poorly tolerated due to unpleasant taste and gastrointestinal side effects such as flatulence, heartburn, and diarrhoea, limiting their clinical utility. A novel enteric-coated fish-oil formulation was developed to protect the capsules against gastric acidity, thereby delivering the n-3 fatty acids to the small intestine. This design aimed to enhance absorption, reduce gastrointestinal intolerance, and improve compliance, making long-term maintenance therapy feasible for patients with Crohn’s disease in remission.

**Study Design**
This was a one-year, multicentre, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial conducted between May 1992 and September 1993 at outpatient clinics in Italy. Seventy-eight patients with Crohn’s disease in remission and at high risk of relapse were randomised in a 1:1 ratio to receive either nine enteric-coated fish-oil capsules daily (providing 2.7 g of n-3 fatty acids, specifically 1.8 g eicosapentaenoic acid and 0.9 g docosahexaenoic acid) or nine identical placebo capsules containing Miglyol 812. The capsules were coated to resist gastric acid for at least 30 minutes and disintegrate within 60 minutes in the small intestine. Randomisation followed a balanced-block scheme, and medications were identically packaged and labelled. Patients were in clinical remission, defined by a Crohn’s Disease Activity Index (CDAI) score ≤150 for three to 24 months, and had at least one elevated inflammatory marker (α1-acid glycoprotein >130 mg/dL, α2-globulins >0.9 g/dL, or erythrocyte sedimentation rate >40 mm/h). Key exclusions included recent use of mesalamine, sulfasalazine, corticosteroids, or immunosuppressants, and prior bowel resection exceeding 1 m unless followed by recurrence and subsequent remission. The primary endpoint was the proportion of patients experiencing a relapse, defined as an increase in CDAI of ≥100 points from baseline with a score >150 persisting for more than two weeks. Secondary endpoints included the proportion maintaining remission at one year and changes in laboratory markers of inflammation. Compliance was assessed by pill counts. Statistical analysis employed the chi-square test for relapse rates, Mann–Whitney U test for continuous variables, Kaplan–Meier life-table analysis with log-rank test for time to relapse, and multivariate logistic regression to assess independent predictors of relapse.

**Patient Population**
A total of 78 patients (39 per treatment group) were enrolled. Baseline demographics and disease characteristics were well balanced between groups. The median age was 34 years in the fish-oil group and 39 years in the placebo group. Each group comprised 19 men and 20 women. The median duration of Crohn’s disease was 66 months in the placebo group and 68 months in the fish-oil group. The median duration of remission before study entry was seven to eight months. Disease distribution (ileal, ileocolonic, colonic) was similar across groups. All patients had achieved their last remission with corticosteroids, mesalamine, or immunosuppressive agents, and 13–14 patients per group had a history of intestinal resection. No significant baseline imbalances between the randomised groups were reported.

**Key Findings**
The primary analysis demonstrated a significantly lower relapse rate in the fish-oil group compared with placebo. Among 39 patients assigned to fish oil, 11 (28%) relapsed, whereas 27 of 39 patients (69%) in the placebo group relapsed. The absolute difference in relapse rates was 41 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], 21 to 61; P <0.001). At the one-year study completion, 23 patients (59%) in the fish-oil group remained in remission, compared with 10 patients (26%) in the placebo group (P = 0.003). Kaplan–Meier analysis confirmed a significantly longer time to relapse in the fish-oil group (P = 0.006 by log-rank test). Multivariate logistic regression, adjusting for sex, age, previous surgery, duration of disease, and smoking status, identified fish-oil treatment as the sole independent predictor of reduced relapse risk. The odds ratio for relapse in the placebo group relative to the fish-oil group was 4.2 (95% CI, 1.6 to 10.7). Secondary laboratory endpoints showed a significant reduction in inflammatory markers (erythrocyte sedimentation rate, α2-globulins, α1-acid glycoprotein) in the fish-oil group compared with an increase in the placebo group. Fatty acid analysis of red-cell phospholipids in patients in remission at study end demonstrated marked incorporation of eicosapentaenoic and docosahexaenoic acids in the fish-oil group, with a corresponding displacement of arachidonic acid.

**Safety & Tolerability**
Adverse events were limited primarily to gastrointestinal intolerance. Four patients (10%) in the fish-oil group discontinued treatment due to diarrhoea, compared with one patient (3%) in the placebo group. In all five cases, diarrhoea onset occurred within the first month of treatment and did not improve with reduction of capsule intake. No other side effects were reported. No serious adverse events were documented. The overall discontinuation rate due to diarrhoea was higher with fish oil, though the majority of patients in this group tolerated the preparation.

**Authors' Conclusions**
The authors conclude that the novel enteric-coated fish-oil preparation is effective in reducing the rate of relapse in patients with Crohn’s disease in remission. They note that the formulation improves tolerability compared with traditional fish oil, as evidenced by high compliance and the specific side effect profile. A limitation acknowledged is that the study did not compare the efficacy of fish oil directly with that of mesalamine, the standard maintenance therapy. The authors suggest that the observed benefit may relate to the anti-inflammatory actions of n-3 fatty acids, including inhibition of leukotriene B4 and thromboxane A2 synthesis, and the high degree of incorporation into cell membranes achieved with this preparation.

**Reference**
Belluzzi A, Brignola C, Campieri M, Pera A, Boschi S, Miglioli M. Effect of an enteric-coated fish-oil preparation on relapses in Crohn’s disease. N Engl J Med 1996; 334:1557-60.

title: Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease
text:
Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease

Andrea Belluzzi, Corrado Brignola, Massimo Campieri, Angelo Perà, Stefano Boschi, and Mario Miglioli. New England Journal of Medicine. 1996;334(24):1557-1560. doi:10.1056/NEJM199606133342401

Background & Rationale
Crohn’s disease is characterised by periods of remission interrupted by relapses, with the highest risk occurring soon after remission is achieved. Patients with elevated acute-phase proteins have an increased risk of relapse. Fish oil has demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties through mechanisms including reduced production of leukotriene B4 and thromboxane A2, inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and free radical scavenging. However, conventional fish oil preparations are poorly tolerated due to gastrointestinal side effects and unpleasant taste. This study evaluated a novel enteric-coated fish-oil preparation designed to improve tolerability while maintaining therapeutic efficacy.

Study Design
This was a one-year, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial conducted between May 1992 and September 1993 at two Italian centres. Seventy-eight patients with established Crohn’s disease in clinical remission were enrolled. Eligibility required a Crohn’s Disease Activity Index score below 150 for at least three months but less than two years, plus evidence of inflammation (elevated serum a1-acid glycoprotein, a2-globulin, or erythrocyte sedimentation rate). Key exclusion criteria included recent use of mesalamine, sulfasalazine, corticosteroids, or immunosuppressive drugs, and extensive bowel resection. Patients were randomised to receive either nine enteric-coated capsules daily containing 2.7 g of n-3 fatty acids (1.8 g eicosapentaenoic acid and 0.9 g docosahexaenoic acid) or identical placebo capsules. The primary endpoint was clinical relapse, defined as an increase in Crohn’s Disease Activity Index score by at least 100 points above baseline and above 150 for more than two weeks. Secondary endpoints included maintenance of remission and changes in inflammatory markers. Randomisation was balanced-block, and blinding was maintained throughout. Statistical analysis used chi-square tests, Kaplan-Meier survival curves, log-rank analysis, and multivariate logistic regression.

Patient Population
The study population comprised 78 patients (39 per group) with a median age of 34-39 years and a male-to-female ratio of approximately 1:1. The median duration of disease was 66-68 months, and the median duration of remission was 7-8 months. Previous bowel resection had been performed in 13-14 patients per group. Baseline characteristics including disease location, smoking status, and Crohn’s Disease Activity Index scores were similar between groups. All patients had evidence of inflammation at baseline, placing them at high risk for relapse.

Key Findings
The relapse rate was significantly lower in the fish-oil group compared to placebo: 11 of 39 patients (28%) versus 27 of 39 patients (69%), representing a 41 percentage point difference (95% CI: 21 to 61; P=0.001). After one year, 23 patients (59%) in the fish-oil group remained in remission compared to 10 patients (26%) in the placebo group (P=0.003). Kaplan-Meier analysis confirmed significantly better maintenance of remission in the fish-oil group (P=0.006). Multivariate logistic regression identified fish-oil treatment as the only significant predictor of relapse (odds ratio 4.2; 95% CI: 1.6 to 10.7), with sex, age, previous surgery, disease duration, and smoking status having no significant effect. Among patients who relapsed, the distribution of disease location and major symptoms were similar between groups.

Safety & Tolerability
Five patients (10%) in the fish-oil group discontinued due to diarrhoea, which began within the first month of treatment and did not improve with dose reduction. One patient in each group was lost to follow-up. No other side effects were reported. Laboratory markers of inflammation (erythrocyte sedimentation rate, serum a2-globulins, and serum a1-acid glycoprotein) showed significant improvement in the fish-oil group compared to placebo. Analysis of red blood cell phospholipid fatty acid composition demonstrated incorporation of n-3 fatty acids and displacement of arachidonic acid in the fish-oil group.

Authors' Conclusions
The authors concluded that the enteric-coated fish-oil preparation significantly reduced the rate of relapse in patients with Crohn’s disease in remission. They noted that the preparation was well-tolerated by most patients and that the high degree of n-3 fatty acid absorption and incorporation into phospholipid membranes contributed to its efficacy. The authors acknowledged that the relative efficacy of this preparation compared to mesalamine, the current standard treatment for maintenance of remission, remains to be determined.

Reference
Belluzzi A, Brignola C, Campieri M, Perà A, Boschi S, Miglioli M. Effect of an enteric-coated fish-oil preparation on relapses in Crohn’s disease. N Engl J Med. 1996;334(24):1557-1560. doi:10.1056/NEJM199606133342401
